era_context: The mid-1780s marked a transitional moment between the end of the American Revolutionary War and the onset of the French Revolution. European powers were recalibrating colonial and diplomatic arrangements after the 1783 peace settlements. The Enlightenment was at its intellectual apex, with Kant, Herder, and others producing landmark philosophical works. The Qing dynasty under the Qianlong Emperor dominated East Asia, while the Ottoman Empire grappled with Russian expansionism following the loss of Crimea. Britain was pivoting from its American losses toward deeper engagement in India, and Japan suffered through the devastating Tenmei famine.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior sub-Saharan Africa beyond coastal trading posts
Aboriginal Australian societies prior to sustained European contact
Pacific Island communities beyond sporadic European encounters
Central Asian steppe polities between Russian and Qing frontiers
Interior Southeast Asian kingdoms beyond court chronicles

events.title: U.S. Congress ratifies the Treaty of Paris
events.description: On 14 January 1784, the Continental Congress, meeting in Annapolis, Maryland, ratified the Treaty of Paris, formally ending the American Revolutionary War. The treaty recognized American independence, established boundaries for the new nation stretching to the Mississippi River, and provided for the withdrawal of British forces. Ratification had been delayed by difficulties in assembling a quorum of state delegations, and the deadline set by the treaty's own terms was narrowly met. The ratified treaty was exchanged with Britain in Paris on 12 May 1784.

sources.name: Journals of the Continental Congress, 14 January 1784
Richard B. Morris, The Peacemakers: The Great Powers and American Independence (1965)

events.certainty_note: Contemporary congressional records document the exact date of ratification.

events.title: Treaty of Constantinople confirms Russian annexation of Crimea
events.description: On 8 January 1784, the Ottoman Empire and the Russian Empire signed the Treaty of Constantinople (also called the Convention of Aynalıkavak), formally recognizing Russia's annexation of the Crimean Khanate, which had been carried out by Catherine the Great in April 1783. The treaty represented a major Ottoman diplomatic humiliation and a fundamental shift in the Black Sea balance of power. Russia gained warm-water ports and a strategic position that would drive Ottoman-Russian conflict for the next century. The loss of Crimea contributed to internal Ottoman reform pressures.
key_figures: Grigory Potemkin (Russian commander and administrator of Crimea)

sources.name: Text of the Convention, Ottoman and Russian diplomatic archives
Alan Fisher, The Russian Annexation of the Crimea, 1772–1783 (1970)
Virginia Aksan, Ottoman Wars 1700–1870 (2007)

events.certainty_note: Treaty text survives in both Ottoman and Russian archives. Date is well established.

events.title: Treaty of Mangalore ends Second Anglo-Mysore War
events.region: Mangalore, Kingdom of Mysore (present-day Karnataka, India)
events.description: On 11 March 1784, the Treaty of Mangalore was signed between Tipu Sultan of Mysore and the British East India Company, ending the Second Anglo-Mysore War (1780–1784). The treaty restored the status quo ante bellum, with both sides returning conquered territories and prisoners. It was the last occasion on which an Indian power dictated terms to the British and was considered a diplomatic humiliation for the Company. The unresolved rivalry between Tipu Sultan and the British would erupt again in the Third Anglo-Mysore War of 1790.

sources.name: Treaty of Mangalore, East India Company records
Mohibbul Hasan, History of Tipu Sultan (1951)
C. A. Bayly, Indian Society and the Making of the British Empire (1988)

events.certainty_note: The treaty text survives in East India Company archives. Date is firmly established.

events.title: Pitt's India Act restructures British governance of India
events.description: On 13 August 1784, the East India Company Act (commonly known as Pitt's India Act) received royal assent. Introduced by Prime Minister William Pitt the Younger, the act created a Board of Control appointed by the Crown to oversee the East India Company's political and military affairs in India, while leaving commercial operations to the Company's directors. This dual system of governance fundamentally reshaped British imperial administration in India and remained the basic framework until the Company's dissolution in 1858. It represented Parliament's response to perceived abuses under Company rule highlighted by the impeachment proceedings against Warren Hastings.
key_figures: Henry Dundas (key political ally, later President of the Board of Control)
Warren Hastings (Governor-General whose conduct prompted reform)

sources.name: East India Company Act 1784 (24 Geo. III, c. 25), Parliamentary Archives
P. J. Marshall, The Making and Unmaking of Empires: Britain, India, and America c.1750–1783 (2005)

events.certainty_note: Parliamentary records provide exact date of royal assent and full text of the statute.
cross_references: 1858-003 (Government of India Act abolishing the Company)

events.title: Kant publishes 'Answering the Question: What is Enlightenment?'
events.description: In December 1784, Immanuel Kant published his essay 'Beantwortung der Frage: Was ist Aufklärung?' in the Berlinische Monatsschrift. The essay defined Enlightenment as humanity's emergence from self-imposed immaturity and advanced the motto 'Sapere aude' — dare to know. Kant argued for the freedom of public reason while accepting constraints on its private (official) use, offering a carefully calibrated defence of intellectual freedom under Frederick the Great's relatively tolerant Prussian regime. The essay became one of the defining texts of the European Enlightenment and remains foundational in political philosophy.

sources.name: Berlinische Monatsschrift, December 1784
James Schmidt, 'The Question of Enlightenment: Kant, Mendelssohn, and the Mittwochsgesellschaft', Journal of the History of Ideas (1989)

events.certainty_note: The original journal publication survives. Exact date of issue is documented.

events.title: Wesley's Deed of Declaration formalises Methodist organisation
events.description: On 28 February 1784, John Wesley enrolled the Deed of Declaration in the Court of Chancery, legally establishing a 'Conference of the People called Methodists' as a body of 100 named preachers with authority to govern the Methodist movement after his death. Later in September 1784, Wesley ordained Thomas Coke as superintendent for America, an act that effectively broke with the Church of England by assuming episcopal authority. These actions in 1784 laid the institutional foundation for Methodism's emergence as an independent denomination, which would become one of the largest Protestant movements globally.
key_figures: Thomas Coke (ordained as superintendent for America)

sources.name: Deed of Declaration, enrolled in Chancery, 28 February 1784
Henry Rack, Reasonable Enthusiast: John Wesley and the Rise of Methodism (1989)

events.certainty_note: Legal document survives in court records. Wesley's ordinations are documented in his journals.

events.title: Shelikhov establishes first permanent Russian settlement in Alaska
events.region: Three Saints Bay, Kodiak Island, Alaska (Russian America)
events.description: In August 1784, Grigory Shelikhov led an expedition that established a settlement at Three Saints Bay on Kodiak Island, founding the first permanent Russian colony in Alaska. The colonists violently subjugated the indigenous Alutiiq people at the Battle of Awa'uq (Refuge Rock), killing hundreds in what became known as one of the worst massacres in Alaskan history. Shelikhov's venture laid the groundwork for the Russian-American Company, chartered in 1799, and for Russia's presence in North America that lasted until the Alaska Purchase of 1867.

sources.name: Grigory Shelikhov, A Voyage to America, 1783–1786 (published 1791)
Lydia Black, Russians in Alaska, 1732–1867 (2004)
Alutiiq oral tradition regarding the Awa'uq massacre

events.certainty_note: Shelikhov's own account and subsequent Russian administrative records confirm the settlement. Archaeological evidence at Three Saints Bay corroborates.

events.title: Tenmei famine devastates Japan
events.region: Japan (especially northern Honshu and Tohoku region)
events.description: The Tenmei famine (1782–1788) reached catastrophic intensity in 1783–1784 following the eruption of Mount Asama in 1783, which blanketed crops with volcanic ash. In 1784, northern Japan continued to suffer extreme crop failures, with the Tohoku region worst affected. Conservative estimates suggest hundreds of thousands died from starvation and disease across the famine period, with some domains losing 30–50% of their population. The famine exposed the structural weaknesses of the Tokugawa bakufu's rice-based economy and prompted significant policy reforms, including famine relief granaries and crop diversification programmes.
key_figures: Tanuma Okitsugu (senior councillor, blamed for inadequate response)

sources.name: Domain administrative records (han records) from Tsugaru, Nambu, and other Tohoku domains
Conrad Totman, Early Modern Japan (1993)
William W. Kelly, Deference and Defiance in Nineteenth-Century Japan (1985)

events.certainty_note: Domain population registers and administrative reports provide detailed documentation. The scale of mortality is well attested though exact figures vary by source.

events.title: New Brunswick established as a separate colony for Loyalist refugees
events.region: British North America (present-day New Brunswick, Canada)
events.description: On 16 August 1784, the British government partitioned the colony of Nova Scotia to create the new Province of New Brunswick, primarily to accommodate the influx of approximately 14,000 Loyalist refugees who had fled the newly independent United States. Cape Breton was also separated from Nova Scotia as a distinct colony at the same time. Thomas Carleton was appointed the first governor. The mass Loyalist migration reshaped the demographics and politics of British North America, and New Brunswick's founding reflected the ongoing geopolitical consequences of the American Revolution across the continent.
key_figures: Thomas Carleton (first Governor of New Brunswick)
Guy Carleton, Lord Dorchester (brother, Governor of Quebec)

sources.name: Order in Council establishing New Brunswick, 16 August 1784, British Colonial Office records
Maya Jasanoff, Liberty's Exiles: American Loyalists in the Revolutionary World (2011)

events.certainty_note: British colonial administrative records provide exact documentation of the partition.
cross_references: 1783-001 (Treaty of Paris ending the American Revolution)

events.title: First mail coach service launched in England
events.description: On 2 August 1784, the first mail coach service ran from Bristol to London, a journey of about 120 miles completed in approximately 16 hours. The service was conceived by John Palmer, a Bath theatre manager, who proposed replacing the slow and robbery-prone mounted post boys with armed, scheduled coach services. The success of the Bristol–London route led to rapid nationwide expansion: by 1785, mail coaches served most major English routes. The system transformed British communications, commerce, and road infrastructure, and the mail coach became a defining feature of Georgian-era transport until displaced by railways in the 1830s.
key_figures: John Palmer (originator of the mail coach system)
William Pitt the Younger (Prime Minister, supporter of the scheme)

sources.name: Post Office records and contemporary press reports, August 1784
Edmund Vale, The Mail-Coach Men of the Late Eighteenth Century (1960)

events.certainty_note: Post Office archives and contemporary newspapers document the date and route precisely.

events.title: Cavendish publishes discovery of water's composition
events.description: In 1784, Henry Cavendish published his paper 'Experiments on Air' in the Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society, reporting that water is composed of 'inflammable air' (hydrogen) and 'dephlogisticated air' (oxygen). Though Cavendish framed his findings within phlogiston theory, his experimental demonstration that water was a compound rather than an element was a landmark in chemistry. The discovery became entangled in a bitter priority dispute with James Watt and Antoine Lavoisier, both of whom had reached similar conclusions around the same period.
key_figures: Antoine Lavoisier (rival claimant, working in Paris)

sources.name: Henry Cavendish, 'Experiments on Air', Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society, Vol. 74 (1784)
Christa Jungnickel and Russell McCormmach, Cavendish: The Experimental Life (1999)

events.certainty_note: The publication in Philosophical Transactions is dated and survives. The priority dispute with Watt and Lavoisier is well documented.

events.title: Treaty of Fort Stanwix with the Iroquois Confederacy
events.region: Fort Stanwix, New York, United States
events.description: On 22 October 1784, commissioners of the United States concluded the Treaty of Fort Stanwix (the second treaty by that name) with representatives of the Six Nations of the Iroquois Confederacy. The treaty compelled the Iroquois — who had largely allied with Britain during the Revolutionary War — to cede extensive lands in western New York and Pennsylvania. The treaty was negotiated under duress, with American commissioners treating the Iroquois as a conquered people rather than as sovereign negotiating partners. Many Iroquois leaders repudiated the treaty, and it generated lasting resentment that fuelled continued frontier conflict.

sources.name: Treaty text, 22 October 1784, U.S. National Archives
Colin Calloway, The American Revolution in Indian Country (1995)
Francis Jennings, The Ambiguous Iroquois Empire (1984)

events.certainty_note: Treaty text and ratification records survive in U.S. National Archives.

events.title: Congress passes the Land Ordinance (Jefferson's western plan)
events.description: On 23 April 1784, the Continental Congress adopted Thomas Jefferson's plan for the governance of western territories, sometimes called the Ordinance of 1784. The ordinance proposed dividing the territories west of the Appalachians into self-governing districts that would eventually be admitted as new states on equal footing with the original thirteen. Though it was superseded by the more detailed Northwest Ordinance of 1787 before being fully implemented, Jefferson's 1784 ordinance established the revolutionary principle that new territories would become co-equal states rather than permanent colonies — a foundational concept in American territorial expansion.

sources.name: Journals of the Continental Congress, 23 April 1784
Peter Onuf, Statehood and Union: A History of the Northwest Ordinance (1987)

events.certainty_note: Congressional records document the ordinance's passage and text.

events.title: Jean-Pierre Blanchard's cross-Channel balloon flight preparations
events.description: Throughout 1784, ballooning fever gripped Europe following the Montgolfier brothers' first manned flights in 1783. Jean-Pierre Blanchard made multiple balloon ascents in France and England during 1784, refining hydrogen balloon technology and experimenting with rudimentary steering mechanisms. On 2 March 1784, Blanchard made his first English ascent from Chelsea. These flights, along with those of other aeronauts across Europe, demonstrated the potential of lighter-than-air travel and drove significant public and scientific interest, culminating in Blanchard's famous cross-Channel flight in January 1785.

sources.name: Contemporary press reports in London and Paris newspapers, 1784
L. T. C. Rolt, The Aeronauts: A History of Ballooning, 1783–1903 (1966)

events.certainty_note: Multiple contemporary newspaper accounts and pamphlets document the ascents with precise dates.

events.title: Tây Sơn forces consolidate control over southern Vietnam
events.description: In 1784, the Tây Sơn brothers continued consolidating their control over southern Vietnam after overthrowing the Nguyễn lords. Nguyễn Ánh, the surviving Nguyễn claimant, secured Siamese military intervention and launched a counterattack, but the Tây Sơn forces under Nguyễn Huệ decisively defeated a combined Siamese-Nguyễn fleet at the Battle of Rạch Gầm–Xoài Mút in January 1785 (preparation and the Siamese invasion occurred in late 1784). This campaign demonstrated the Tây Sơn's military effectiveness and temporarily ended Siamese interference in Vietnamese politics.
key_figures: Nguyễn Ánh (Nguyễn claimant, future Emperor Gia Long)
Taksin's successor Rama I (Siamese king who authorised the intervention)

sources.name: Đại Việt sử ký toàn thư and later Vietnamese court chronicles
George Dutton, The Tây Sơn Uprising: Society and Rebellion in Eighteenth-Century Vietnam (2006)

events.certainty_note: Vietnamese and Siamese sources corroborate the campaign timeline. The decisive battle at Rạch Gầm–Xoài Mút occurred in January 1785, but the Siamese invasion that provoked it was launched in late 1784.

events.title: Bodawpaya consolidates power in Burma
events.description: King Bodawpaya, who seized the Burmese throne in February 1782 by overthrowing his nephew, spent 1783–1784 consolidating his rule over the Konbaung kingdom. He suppressed rival claimants and began ambitious administrative and religious reforms, including a major land survey and an attempt to purify Theravada Buddhist practice. Bodawpaya would go on to conquer Arakan in 1784–1785, bringing the Mahamuni Buddha image to his capital — an act of enormous religious and political significance. His expansionist policies would eventually bring Burma into conflict with British India.

sources.name: Glass Palace Chronicle (Hmannan Yazawin), compiled 1829
Victor Lieberman, Strange Parallels: Southeast Asia in Global Context, c. 800–1830 (2003)

events.certainty_note: Burmese chronicles were compiled later but are broadly reliable for this period. The Arakan conquest is dated to 1784–1785 in most sources.

events.title: Herder publishes Ideas on the Philosophy of the History of Mankind
events.description: In 1784, Johann Gottfried Herder published the first volume of Ideen zur Philosophie der Geschichte der Menschheit (Ideas on the Philosophy of the History of Mankind), a sweeping attempt to trace human development from cosmological origins through the emergence of diverse cultures. Herder argued that each culture (Volk) had its own unique genius and developmental trajectory, rejecting Enlightenment universalism's tendency to measure all peoples against a single European standard. The work profoundly influenced Romanticism, cultural nationalism, the development of anthropology, and modern concepts of cultural relativism.
key_figures: Johann Wolfgang von Goethe (friend and intellectual interlocutor in Weimar)

sources.name: Johann Gottfried Herder, Ideen zur Philosophie der Geschichte der Menschheit, Vol. 1 (1784)
Michael Forster, After Herder: Philosophy of Language in the German Tradition (2010)

events.certainty_note: First edition is dated 1784. Publication records confirm.
cross_references: 1784-005 (Kant's 'What is Enlightenment?' — part of the same intellectual moment)

events.title: Joseph II's administrative and religious reforms in the Habsburg lands
events.region: Habsburg Empire (Austria, Bohemia, Hungary, Austrian Netherlands)
events.description: Throughout 1784, Emperor Joseph II continued his ambitious programme of enlightened absolutist reform. He imposed German as the sole language of administration across the multi-ethnic empire (provoking fierce resistance in Hungary and the Austrian Netherlands), suppressed additional contemplative monasteries, and extended religious toleration beyond the 1781 Edict. He also abolished serfdom in Hungary in August 1785 (building on the 1781 Bohemian reforms), though many measures of 1784 prepared the ground. These reforms generated severe backlash that would contribute to revolts in the Austrian Netherlands and Hungary before Joseph's death in 1790.
key_figures: Joseph II (Holy Roman Emperor and Habsburg ruler)

sources.name: Imperial decrees and administrative correspondence, Habsburg Haus-, Hof- und Staatsarchiv
Derek Beales, Joseph II, Volume 2: Against the World, 1780–1790 (2009)

events.certainty_note: Imperial administrative records provide detailed documentation of specific reform decrees.

events.title: Maratha Confederacy struggles with internal power dynamics
events.region: Western and Central India, Maratha Confederacy
events.description: Following the Treaty of Salbai (1782) that ended the First Anglo-Maratha War, the Maratha Confederacy in 1784 continued to grapple with the tension between the Peshwa in Pune and the increasingly autonomous Maratha chiefs — the Scindias of Gwalior, the Holkars of Indore, the Bhonsles of Nagpur, and the Gaekwads of Baroda. Mahadji Scindia in particular was extending his influence in northern India, acting as regent for the Mughal Emperor Shah Alam II in Delhi. This fragmentation weakened the Confederacy's collective capacity and would prove disastrous in later conflicts with the British.
key_figures: Peshwa Madhav Rao II (nominal head of the Confederacy)
Shah Alam II (Mughal Emperor under Scindia's protection)

sources.name: Marathi administrative documents (akhbarat) from Pune archives
Stewart Gordon, The Marathas 1600–1818 (1993)

events.certainty_note: Marathi-language administrative records survive for this period. The political dynamics are well attested.

events.title: Benjamin Franklin leads Franco-American commission on animal magnetism
events.description: In 1784, King Louis XVI appointed a royal commission to investigate the claims of Franz Anton Mesmer, whose theory of 'animal magnetism' (mesmerism) had taken Parisian society by storm. The commission, chaired by Benjamin Franklin and including the chemist Antoine Lavoisier and the physician Joseph-Ignace Guillotin, conducted controlled experiments — among the earliest uses of blinding and placebo controls in medical investigation. The commission's August 1784 report concluded that mesmerism's effects were due to imagination rather than any physical fluid, establishing a landmark in the history of evidence-based medicine and experimental psychology.
key_figures: Benjamin Franklin (U.S. Minister to France, commission chair)
Franz Anton Mesmer (proponent of animal magnetism)

sources.name: Rapport des commissaires chargés par le roi de l'examen du magnétisme animal (1784)
Robert Darnton, Mesmerism and the End of the Enlightenment in France (1968)

events.certainty_note: The commission's published report survives. Franklin's correspondence about the investigation is also extant.

disconfirming_evidence: The Treaty of Constantinople (1784-002) is sometimes dated to 1783 in older sources, and the distinction between the 1783 annexation and the 1784 formal Ottoman recognition can be conflated. Some historians argue the treaty was less a separate diplomatic achievement than a formality confirming a fait accompli. Regarding the Treaty of Mangalore (1784-003), the characterisation of it as the 'last treaty dictated by an Indian power to the British' is a widely repeated claim but somewhat oversimplified — the treaty was a mutual restoration of status quo ante bellum rather than a one-sided dictation, and British strategic position was not as weak as the traditional narrative suggests, since they retained key coastal positions. For the Ordinance of 1784 (1784-013), Jefferson's original draft included a clause banning slavery in western territories after 1800, which failed by a single vote — some historians argue this near-miss was less significant than commonly portrayed, since enforcement would have been impractical regardless. The description of the Franklin commission on mesmerism (1784-020) as pioneering blinded experiments is broadly accepted, but historians of science note that earlier informal blinding tests existed, and the commission's methods were less systematic than modern accounts sometimes imply.
historiographic_note: 1784 falls within one of the best-documented periods of world history for Europe and the Americas, with extensive governmental archives, published scientific and philosophical works, and rich newspaper coverage. The historiographic record is heavily weighted toward Euro-American perspectives, particularly British, French, and American sources. South and Southeast Asian events are documented but often filtered through colonial or later nationalist lenses. Japanese records for this period are strong but were historically underutilised in Western scholarship. The Tây Sơn period in Vietnam relies partly on later dynastic chronicles written by the victorious Nguyễn dynasty, which had reason to diminish the Tây Sơn legacy. Sub-Saharan African and Pacific histories for this year remain largely invisible in the documentary record, though oral traditions and archaeological work continue to expand our knowledge.

graph_edges.note: Ratification of the Treaty of Paris follows its signing in September 1783
Treaty of Constantinople formalises the 1783 Russian annexation of Crimea
Unresolved tensions from the Treaty of Mangalore led to the Third Anglo-Mysore War
Pitt's India Act established the governance framework that lasted until the Company's dissolution
Kant's Enlightenment essay builds on the epistemological framework of the First Critique
First Russian settlement in Alaska leads to Russian America, ending with the 1867 Alaska Purchase
The 1784 famine intensification followed the 1783 eruption of Mount Asama
Loyalist resettlement and New Brunswick's creation were direct consequences of the American Revolution's end
Fort Stanwix treaty was part of the series of post-war treaties with Native nations leading to the Northwest Ordinance
Jefferson's 1784 Ordinance was superseded by and directly influenced the Northwest Ordinance of 1787
Siamese invasion of late 1784 led to the decisive Tây Sơn victory at Rạch Gầm–Xoài Mút in January 1785
Herder's cultural pluralism and Kant's universalist Enlightenment essay published the same year represent contrasting philosophical visions
The mesmerism commission reflects the same spirit of empirical investigation that drove the balloon experiments
